etymology_and_history.root_language: Language**: From "glee" from Old English "glīw" (mirth, joy)
etymology_and_history.original_meaning: Meaning**: Mirth joy; having excited jubilation and glee excitement with jubilant presence
etymology_and_history.semantic_evolution: Evolution**: Mirth joy → excited jubilation → contemporary glee excitement and glee jubilation with excited presence
etymology_and_history.cultural_shifts: Shifts**: Mirth joy → excited jubilation → glee excitement → excited understanding

contemporary_usage.primary_definition: Definition**: Having glee excitement and glee jubilation; experiencing excited jubilation and glee glee with glee presence
contemporary_usage.colloquial_usage: Usage**: "Filled with glee," "glee at success," "dancing with glee"
contemporary_usage.register: Positive; indicates excitement and jubilant celebration with triumphant excitement
